data.title: Defensive, Sector-Based Dividend Growth: 6 Opportunities And One Special Highlight (Part 1)
data.description:
Dividend growth investing has never rung a bell with me. If you need dividends, a .5% from something like Google (GOOGL) is likely not enough. On the other hand, British American Tobacco's (BTI) over 10% yield last year was a singularly supreme high-yield opportunity, which represents 20x the yield Google offers.

Restricting oneself to dividend growth stocks has seemed like a way to hamstring all one's opportunities in an investment landscape of 50,000 stocks.

Despite this, I have found the quality offered by companies with small dividends growing in the 10-15% range has often been quite impressive. In fact, if I had to say one thing about where quality generally lies, apart from pointing someone to my Five Factor Model, I would suggest that some of the highest quality stocks are low-yield dividend growth companies.

I recently published an article detailing most of my portfolio, which focuses on concentrated bets on the highest quality companies I can find.

  • A Question Of Quality, If I Could Only Own Six Stocks

As stated in the article, this represents 75.6% of my portfolio. I'd like to review some of the remainder of my holdings, which I consider to be a small diversification away from these main six holdings. There are five companies in this bucket - they are sector-diversified, and have incredibly strong histories of revenue, earnings, and dividend growth. They also exhibit aligned management, ultra-strong balance sheets and are generally regarded as having worthy moats.

One would think these are hidden gems that others haven't found yet. On the contrary, they are widely known, large cap American companies, and yet in today's market they are still somewhat unloved by the investing public. It seems there will always be a little room for occasional equities mispricing, even in the "overfished" US equity markets.

Here are the five companies. They represent roughly 10% of my portfolio, and it's likely in the next few weeks this could bump to 12% through more additions.

1. MSCI (MSCI)

MSCI is an index and ETF provider, a financial data analytics platform and a financial research hub. MSCI is deeply imbedded in the investing community, especially among large institutions and hedge funds.

My initial inspiration for MSCI came from Warren Buffett's long-time position in Moody's (MCO). The basic premise: If you believe that American capitalism will prevail, then the public pricing of those businesses, through the US stock market, will also prevail. As such, indices, ETFs, ESG ratings, analysis, financial data, and financial applications will continue to be needed into the very long-term by the aforementioned groups.

MSCI in particular has a history of nearly 20% EPS CAGR over the last ten years, and over that period valuations have remained very stable, with a beta of 0.94. Notably, MSCI's key metrics are as follows:

  • EPS CAGR of 20% over the past 10 years
  • Sales growth expected in the 9-10% range, from a 3Y average of 11.8%
  • 3.3% inside ownership
  • 5Y dividend growth rate of 20%
  • Outstanding shares falling consistently from 98.8M in 2015 to 77.7M last year.
  • Dividend yield of 1.25%

Unlike competitors Moody's and S&P Global (SPGI) which are near all-time highs and valuations, MSCI is trading somewhat below its ten-year average valuation, perhaps given near-term economic uncertainties.

I believe this is a buy here (although not a strong one) for investors seeking capital preservation and substantial capital gains.

2. Intuit (INTU)

Intuit is another company, in a different sector, which has and is expected to continue to benefit from long-term tailwinds. Intuit is an automatic tax filing software service, offering business and personal tax services, now hosted on the cloud on a subscription basis. The general thesis is as follows: as a company or individual files their taxes year after year through the same provider, the history of tax filings grows. Especially for businesses, having all filings for all years of your business in the same place is extremely helpful.

Further, aside from the legacy TurboTax and QuickBooks products, Intuit offers Mint, a personal finance software product and Credit Karma, acquired in 2020 to offer credit monitoring.

Intuit's focus is on developing further inroads to small business and personal finance, with an expansion to payroll and email marketing, as well as AI automation, efficiencies, and insights for both individual and business users.

Intuit's fundamentals also stack up well.

  • EPS CAGR of 21% over the past 10 years
  • Sales growth expected in the 12-13% range, from a 3Y average of 17.2%
  • 2.4% inside ownership
  • 5Y dividend growth rate of 14.2%
  • Outstanding shares have not budged significantly in the last decade, but I believe in the medium-term, share buybacks could become a key capital return strategy for shareholders, along with the current 0.72% dividend yield.

Importantly, Intuit is trading at a fairly depressed valuation compared with historic market expectations.

On Intuit's suite of products (some of which I use myself), strong history of revenue and earnings growth, insider ownership and dividend growth, I consider Intuit to be approaching a Strong Buy. I hope to detail this great company at some point soon in its own article.

3. Elevance Health (ELV)

I mentioned at the beginning of this article that the purpose of these companies in my portfolio was to provide a counter-balance to the concentrated technology investments highlighted in my recent article.

Perhaps nothing could be further diversified from technology as health insurance. Elevance is one of the leading health insurers and services companies in the United States, outdone by size only by UnitedHealth (UNH). Elevance provides health insurance to 46 million Americans through various channels.

On top of that, Elevance offers health services through its Carelon segment. This includes pharmacy benefits management (PBMs), complex care management, and behavioral health solutions. This virtuous circle aids Americans in getting healthy while hopefully lowering health insurance claims.

On the fundamentals side, Elevance proves itself to be a worthy addition to this list and my portfolio.

  • EPS CAGR of 14% over the past 10 years
  • Sales growth expected in the 8-10% range, from a 3Y average of 8.4%
  • $93M inside ownership, which, although small,
  • 5Y dividend growth rate of 15.3%
  • Outstanding shares have not budged significantly in the last decade, but I believe in the medium-term, share buybacks could become a key capital return strategy for shareholders, along with the current 0.72% dividend yield.

Elevance is also suffering in share price, offering investors a moderately discounted opportunity, which, when compared to its historic fluctuations, is actually quite severe.

Summary

As the first trio in this two-part series, MSCI, INTU, and ELV comprise a diversified and quality set of dividend growers with strong insider holdings, a strong history of company growth, reliable growing dividends, and current and potential share buybacks.

As a means to add some diversification from my otherwise concentrated technology portfolio, I rate these three stable EPS growers in relatively moated positions buys.

In the second part of this series, I intend to highlight three more companies with similar characteristics.

data.title: Comstock Resources: First Quarter Will Be Better Still
data.description:
Comstock Resources (NYSE:CRK) operates in the Haynesville. The Haynesville is the highest cost dry gas basin that is still considered a viable option to develop. Hence, it is known as the swing basin. While the company reported lower production, it also reported improving natural gas prices from this very cold winter. This is one company that will have dramatically improved fortunes from the seasonally strong winter prices that are resulting from continuing La Niña cold spells. It will also benefit from its location near the rapidly expanding export capacity of North America.

Reported Earnings Calculation

Rising prices meant there was an unrealized derivative loss that dominated the income statement as shown below:

As shown above, earnings for the fourth quarter would have been positive without the unrealized "mark to market" derivative loss. It is best to think of derivatives as sales made in advance using a certain benchmark for those sales. That natural gas can still be directed to stronger markets while settling contracts for the benchmark price. But the main goal is to assure a minimal cash flow during times of weak pricing. That is particularly important when operating in a higher cost basin.

For this reason, the derivative contract losses and gains are best thought of as an opportunity cost to realize a "guaranteed" cash flow relatively speaking rather than waiting for (and selling at) market prices for the quarter in question. The actual gain or loss (and cash flow) is determined when the contracts settle.

Hopefully, management can use this period of strong prices to add some favorable derivative contracts just in case prices weaken again when the winter is over.

Operating Costs And Revenue Detail

The actual settlement of the derivatives resulted in a better price than the company could obtain in selling the gas at market prices.

Note also that the cash production costs are very favorable compared to much of the industry. So, in a cyclical downturn like the present time, existing production will cash flow at some low natural gas prices.

The problem has always been the cost of the wells. So, the depreciation figure is on the high side. This limits the cost recovery in this dry gas basin compared to other basins. It also accounts for the "swing basin" status of the Haynesville.

This may also be the basin that benefits the most from the expanding capacity of North America to export natural gas. Should North America natural gas prices join the far stronger world natural gas pricing market as a result of those exports, this is the basin that would increase profitability quite a bit.

Last Article

The last article spent some time discussing the details of the "swing basin" status of the Haynesville.

Comstock Resources has offset some of the high-cost disadvantage of the basin by acquiring acreage back around 2019 through 2022 (very roughly). Much of the acreage was acquired at the time when there were relatively no buyers.

As a result, the company could well have a location advantage of at least a $1 million per well (and likely more) compared to other operators in the area. This advantage would show is corporate profitability because land does not depreciate. Instead, the measurement focuses upon how much capital and debt was needed per well location. Here the answer is likely the lowest in the basin. Less capital per well-used equals less leveraged needed and more profitability during times of decent natural gas prices.

In the latest quarterly report, management was again acquiring acreage during times of weak prices. The negotiations probably began this past summer when prices "went through the floor". The results were seen in the fourth quarter when prices began to rise.

What many do not realize is that profitability is determined by keeping all costs at a reasonable level. There is little to be done about the expensive well cost situation. Therefore, management has instead instituted a low-cost acreage acquisition strategy.

This still means that the company idled rigs when it is not cost-effective to drill wells at current gas prices. But profitability will likely be higher here than at competitors when the inevitable cyclical recovery begins.

Operating Cash Flow

This management obviously chose an aggressive drilling strategy during a period of low prices. This could result in a cost advantage when the cyclical recovery occurs. Now it did mean that the company had to sell stock to keep finances reasonable (as past articles noted). But drilling when costs are low could prove to be competitively advantageous.

As noted before, this is a well-placed gamble that more production is better than less production during a cyclical recovery. The risk, of course, is that the anticipated recovery could be delayed or possibly not occur.

Financial leverage did rise even with the sale of stock that occurred in the past year. The low operating costs shown before make it a very good bet that higher financial leverage will come down very quickly.

Both January and February have proven so far to be colder than normal. This may enable the company to "cash in" a bit early on the wells drilled during a time of weak prices.

Reserves

A quick detour to talk about the reserve report shown below:

During times of relatively weak natural gas prices, reserves are going to come off the reserve report as shown above because some reserves are now too costly to produce. Management did not lose those reserves (as they are still there). It is simply not competitive to develop the acreage where those wells are at the present time.

This is yet another characteristic of a swing basin. The reserves lost in the current report are likely to be at least partially recovered in future reports during periods of stronger commodity prices.

Summary

Management was busy in fiscal year 2024 despite the weak commodity prices.

Note that for the wells drilled and completed, costs came down substantially. Some of that will be permanent savings from technology advances, and some will reverse when basin activity levels climb during the next natural gas price recovery. In the meantime, those wells drilled are likely to have a substantial cost advantage that will result in an average lower depreciation rate for those wells.

Whether this basin would ever be able to have costs that are equal to a lower cost basin like the Marcellus (for example) is an open question. Low-cost basins have switched locations in the past as technology advances. It could therefore happen again in the future.

Comstock Resources remains a strong buy with unusually good management in a higher cost basin. This management has saved money in every possible place it can, and it continues to save money compared to many competitors.

The higher cost wells are a competitive disadvantage that is unlikely to disappear any time soon. But the other things management have been doing to minimize that disadvantage are an example of "going the extra mile" that many managements would ignore.

Risks

This management has a somewhat aggressive strategy financially, as shown by the negative cash flow during a time of weak natural gas prices. The financial leverage rose at a time when most companies would be decreasing financial leverage while waiting for a cyclical recovery. This stock is therefore not for the risk-averse.

Any upstream company is subject to the volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices. A delay in an anticipated recovery or a severe and sustained downturn of commodity prices could materially change the future outlook of the company.

This company is particularly exposed to the downside through the rise in financial leverage. Now, that leverage is not so bad that everything has to go right. But it is clearly an aggressive financial leverage that few companies would try during a cyclical downturn.

A loss of key personnel could have a material effect upon the future of the company.

Fund performance

Institutional Class shares of Columbia Contrarian Core Fund (MUTF:SMGIX) returned 2.37% (net of fees) during the fourth quarter, underperforming the fund’s benchmark, the Russell 1000 Index, which returned 2.75%. U.S. equities generated another quarterly gain in the fourth quarter, adding to the Russell 1000’s substantial annual return in 2024.

Market overview

The U.S. stock market gained 2.75% in the fourth quarter, based on the large-cap Russell 1000 Index, bringing its return for the full year to 24.51%. The November presential election result removed a long-standing source of uncertainty for investors, and it raised hopes that government policy would shift in a more pro-growth, market-friendly direction. In addition, U.S. equities continued to attract investor capital as the nation’s economic growth remained well above that of its global developed-market peers. These factors helped propel stocks to a positive return despite indications that the U.S. Federal Reserve is unlikely to cut interest rates significantly in the year ahead.

The shift in expectations toward a less accommodative Fed played a role in the continued outperformance of growth stocks. As was the case throughout 2024, U.S. mega-cap technology companies — particularly those expected to capitalize on the increased adoption of Artificial Intelligence — remained the primary driver of both broader market returns and relative strength of the growth style. The Russell 1000 Growth Index returned 7.07% in the quarter, outpacing the -1.98% return of the Russell 1000 Value Index. The two indices returned 33.36% and 14.37%, respectively, for the full year.

In another continuation of a trend that was in place in the year’s first three quarters, large-cap stocks outperformed midcaps (as measured by the 0.62% return of the Russell MidCap Index) and small caps (as measured by the 0.33% return of the Russell 2000 Index). Smaller companies are typically more rate-sensitive than large caps, and they benefited less from the robust inflows into passive investments.

Quarterly portfolio recap

  • The fund slightly underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000 Index, during the fourth quarter.
  • The underperformance, relative to the Russell 1000, was mainly attributable to stock selection; sector allocation proved beneficial, partially offsetting the effects from security selection.
  • Stock selection detracted most within information technology, materials and health care.
  • By sector, stock selection in consumer discretionary made the largest relative contribution to returns, followed by selection in financials and industrials.
  • The portfolio’s overweight to communication services contributed the most from a sector allocation standpoint, followed by underweights to real estate and materials.
  • Conversely, the portfolio’s underweight to consumer discretionary was a modest detractor.

Sector weights (%): fund vs. benchmark as of December 31, 2024

Relative contributors included:

  • Tapestry, Inc. (TPR)– Tapestry stock posted a strong return in the third quarter in what proved to be an interesting period for the fashion brand. Toward the end of October it was announced that the Federal Trade Commission ('FTC') was successful in its bid to block the pending merger of Tapestry and Capri, as a U.S. judge sided with the FTC’s case that the merger would eliminate fierce competition and likely lead to unfairly high prices for consumers. Despite Tapestry being on the wrong side of the decision, shares rose sharply higher following the court’s ruling. The stock entered November on positive footing and its upward march would continue after management raised its full-year outlook for fiscal 2025 and also announced a new $2 billion share repurchase program. A week later shares saw an even bigger boost when both Tapestry and Capri announced the mutual decision to officially terminate their proposed $8.5B merger due to legal hurdles. Following the announcement, analysts were quick to announce increases to their Tapestry price targets and affirm their buy/outperform ratings, as it seems both investors and Wall Street analyst’s street feel bullish about the company continuing its own independent journey without Capri.
  • Take-Two Interactive Software, Inc. (TTWO)– Take-Two Interactive posted a solid gain over the period, supported by its latest earnings release as well as a series of positive analyst activities around the stock. The company’s second-quarter bookings and profit both exceeded the Wall Street analyst’s expectations, as the video game publisher continued to benefit from strong performance from its Borderlands and Grand Theft Auto franchises. Take-Two also continued to see healthy in-game purchase activity in its mobile titles alongside its console titles. There was some negative news to contend with as the company’s forecast for third-quarter bookings was slightly below estimates, as a result of a lag in consumer spending. However, much of the excitement and focus on the stock continued to center around the highly anticipated release of the company’s next Grand Theft Auto (GTA) installment, which analysts are expecting to account for much of Take-Two’s future growth. Management affirmed the next GTA title’s fall 2025 release date during its earnings call, much to the delight of investors and gamers alike.
  • Block, Inc. (XYZ)– Block was another top relative contributor over the period, as shares reached a new year-to-date high in November. The company reported Q3 results earlier in the month and shares took an interesting ride following the announcement. Results were a bit mixed, as the company would post a pretty solid quarter with EBITDA growing by almost 70% year over year, but simultaneously also issue disappointing guidance for Q4 gross profit growth. At first the news took a toll on the stock, as shares moved immediately lower in after-hours trading. But the next trading day, Block made an impressive comeback and shares finished substantially higher. While investors weren’t initially pleased with the lower Q4 profit guidance, that news seemed to mostly fade into the background. Instead, what would ultimately help win the day for the stock and fuel its positive month ahead was the promising results of its digital wallet service, Cash App, which saw monthly transacting users grow by 11% in Q3 compared to 5% in the previous quarter. Shares would climb higher in the final month of the quarter after Block reported a 17% year-over-year increase in transactions during the Black Friday-Cyber Monday weekend — a strong start to the holiday shopping season.

Relative detractors included:

  • Elevance Health, Inc. (ELV)– Elevance had been a strong performer throughout much of 2024 but the stock faced some selling pressure in the fourth quarter. The company released Q3 earnings in October with EPS missing consensus estimates and guidance being taken down. Elevance has experienced more pressure in Medicaid than the company was expecting, which has weighed on margins and earnings alike. Medicaid pressures also contributed to management trimming 2024 guidance as well as 2025 EPS, as they see those pressures persisting into next year, but then likely moderating come second half of 2025. These lower-than-expected results sent shares lower following the earnings release and the stock would mostly stay on that trend to finish the period. Despite the pullback and the Medicaid headwind, other segments of Elevance’s business are still showing signs of strength and the company’s recent round of layoffs should also help Elevance weather recent pressures.
  • Newmont Corporation (NEM)– Newmont was the portfolio’s top relative contributor in the third quarter but the stock experienced a pullback in the final quarter of 2024. While the sharp increase in gold prices this year has helped the mining company’s share price, higher-than-expected internal costs weighed on earnings and ultimately led the company to miss EPS consensus estimates for Q3 back in October. Similarly, one of Newmont’s competitors, Barrick Gold, reported its earnings in early November and also missed earnings estimates, citing higher costs. The competitor’s results put Newmont’s higher costs back in focus and weighed on the stock. Later in the month, it was announced that Newmont would be selling its Musselwhite Gold Mine in Ontario to Orla Mining at a deal valued at $850m. While news of the sale lifted Newmont’s shares higher, it wouldn’t be enough to keep it from generating another negative return in November. As a result of the higher production costs the company is facing as well as its lower production expectations going forward, the team decided to exit its position in Newmont in December.
  • American Tower Corporation (AMT)– American Tower was another detractor during the period as the real estate sector was the third worst-performing cohort in the third quarter. The stock’s pullback started in October after the company reported Q3 results that missed expectations. While the REIT’s fund from operations (FOF) still managed to beat Wall Street analyst’s estimates, Q3 revenues underwhelmed, leading the stock to retreat. American Tower leases space to wireless service providers and radio/television broadcasters, and an uncertain economy has caused these companies to lower their spending. This reduction in spending led to lower leasing activity and a decrease in demand for tower space offered by AMT, weighing on revenue. Further volatility around the stock would persist in the two remaining months of the quarter. While the stock initially fell following the results of the U.S. presidential election, AMT and other real estate names would rebound in reaction to Presential-elect Trump’s pick for secretary of housing and urban development. This rally would be short-lived though, as shares would ultimately finish lower due to a slightly hotter November inflation print and the Federal reserve’s more hawkish-than-expected commentary on the future interest rate cuts.

Positioning and outlook

As the 2024 calendar year came to a close, the focus continues to be on the resilience and health of the U.S. economy. While the lagged impact of earlier interest rate increases still poses a potential risk to markets, this risk has been mitigated by the Federal Reserve’s easing activity through the second half of the year. However, uncertainties surrounding another Trump presidency, as well as the ongoing geopolitical tensions, remain. The Contrarian Core portfolio is fairly balanced with regard to various economic scenarios, and our Contrarian Core team sees opportunities in the market, focusing on companies that can continue to grow earnings in a potentially tough economic environment.

data.title: LVMH: Only Hermès Can Rival In Luxury Fashion Prestige
data.description:
LVMH Moët Hennessy - Louis Vuitton, Société Européenne (OTCPK:LVMHF) is the undeniable king of luxury. The French luxury company sells luxury fashion with brands such as Louis Vuitton, Dior, Loro Piana and others, perfumes and cosmetics with Acqua di Parma, Fenty Beauty, Guerlain and others, watches and jewelry with Tiffany & Co, TAG Heuer, and Hublot, and wines and spirits with brands such as Hennessy, Moët & Chandon, and others. The company's presence ranges across the world.

While LVMH also has good positions in wine and spirits, jewelry, and in other luxury goods, the company's ultimate dominance is highlighted by a strong performance in luxury fashion. LVMH's portfolio in high-end fashion has expanded its sales across markets and brands, contributing to the stock's CAGR of 15.0% in the past decade despite a recent slowdown in global luxury good sales.

Only Hermès Can Challenge LVMH's Luxury Fashion Podium

With a long history of acquisitions and brand building, LVMH has cemented its leading position in luxury fashion, becoming the undeniable king in luxury apparel with fashion & leather goods revenues reaching a total of €41.1 billion in 2024, generating €15.2 billion in operating income from the fashion segment alone at an incredible margin of 37.1%.

Cementing the position more, LVMH has continued to outperform most peers in luxury apparel - for example the most notable competing fashion conglomerate Kering (OTCPK:PPRUF) has struggled in maintaining a premium brand image for Gucci and the competitor's many other brands, and Ralph Lauren (RL) had similar issues in prior years, which LVMH has capitalized on through the company's wide portfolio of brands and a stable, healthy brand image. Comparing to publicly traded luxury fashion companies' fashion revenues, not encompassing the entire industry but a major part of it, LVMH has expanded its market share to approximately 39% in 2024 from 29% in early 2019 with the rise of Dior's brand relevance among other growing brands and Louis Vuitton's stable position.

Beyond owned fashion brands, LVMH, for example, owns airport retailer DFS and luxury ecommerce retailer 24S, bringing sales channels to the company's own brands and a way to capitalize on many competitors' sales.

LVMH has also positioned itself well to capitalize well on growth in different markets, as for example, China's long-term growth is expected to be rapid at a 7.3% 2025-2029 industry CAGR. LVMH generates around 36% of its fashion revenues from Asia, excluding Japan, highlighting significant exposure to the Chinese market's good long-term prospects.

As a looming threat, Hermès (OTCPK:HESAY) has rivaled LVMH's Louis Vuitton with a focus on the super-wealthy. The competitor has rapidly gained market share, showing rapid growth across markets and most segments in 2024, even growing Leather Goods & Saddlery revenues by 16.4% in the year against weak overall industry trends. Hermès has previously held a smaller share of the luxury fashion market, but the company's continued growth has now positioned the brand to pose a considerable threat against LVMH's handbag sales in specific. My estimate for LVMH's luxury fashion revenue market share has stagnated in recent quarters, already reaching the current 39% level in early 2023 despite Kering still making way for market share gains afterward - the rise of Hermès and the competitor's Birkin bag has already put a dent in LVMH's market share gains, and the continued rivalry could shake LVMH despite its still clearly leading industry position.

LVMH has already previously fought a battle with the families owning the majority of Hermès. LVMH acquired up to 23.2% ownership in Hermès back in 2013 in an attempt to acquire the rival in a hostile takeover. Hermès ultimately won the battle, with major shareholders pooling majority ownership in a private holding company, blocking any potential takeover.

LVMH's Position in Other Luxury Goods is Notable But Contested

Although LVMH's reach is far beyond luxury fashion alone, LVMH's dominant position is limited to the segment. LVMH's market share in watches, jewelry, fragrance and cosmetics, and in the wines & spirits segment isn't leading, leaving more space for competition to pressure LVMH.

In watches and jewelry, LVMH's brands aren't as well positioned - Hublot and TAG Heuer hold a minor position against the Swiss watch industry's total sales against Richemont's (OTCPK:CFRHF) Cartier and many very highly regarded smaller luxury brands, Swatch Group's (OTCPK:SWGAY) Omega, and many major privately held brands such as Rolex, Audemars Piguet, and others. Richemont is also creating major competition in jewelry, taking major market from LVMH in the watch & jewelry segment; LVMH's share of revenues has continuously declined against Richemont's and Hermès' growth, only temporarily offset in 2021 by the acquisition of Tiffany & Co. The segment still generated €10.6 billion in revenues and €1.5 billion in operating income in 2024 at a 14.6% margin, but the performance was on a notable decline.

LVMH's wines & spirits revenues were €5.9 billion in 2024 with an operating income of €1.4 billion, with sales up slightly from €5.6 billion in 2019 but operating income down from €1.7 billion; the company has lost earnings in the segment with competition against Diageo (DEO), Pernod Ricard (PDRDF), and Brown-Forman (BF.B) among others, with all of the mentioned competing spirit companies outpacing LVMH's earnings growth performance in the past few years and showing better sales growth performances - LVMH has many luxurious spirit and wine brands in its portfolio, but has ultimately been unable to compete against very strong multinational companies that are solely focused on alcohol products.

In perfumes and cosmetics, LVMH's sales performance has been better with revenues reaching €8.4 billion in 2024 from €6.8 billion in 2019, whereas operating income has still stayed stagnant at €0.7 billion despite quite a strong sales performance. For example, Estée Lauder (EL) continues to lead LVMH in cosmetic sales, despite the company's more mixed results in the past couple of years. More notably than the conglomerate's own brands, LVMH operates over 3000 Sephora stores, retailing cosmetics better than producing successful brands.

In conclusion, as the performances in especially watches, jewelry, and alcohol products show, LVMH's core strength lies in fashion. Although the company also generates notable earnings in the smaller segments, LVMH's market share performance is pressured by much more contested markets and often stronger brand portfolios with Richemont's brands in watches and jewelry and for example Diageo's large spirit brand portfolio. Luxury fashion still made up 75% of LVMH's 2024 operating income, making the best-performing segment the company's clearly most important one.

Eying a Better 2025 After Slow Luxury Market

Ultimately, regardless of a weaker competitive position in categories beyond luxury fashion, the company's total performance has been fantastic over the long term with revenues growing at a 10.1% CAGR from 2007 to 2024 with the operating margin mostly hovering above 20%, ending up at €84.7 billion in 2024 revenues at a margin of 23.1%. The luxury goods market has grown at an approximate 5% CAGR over a similar period, highlighting LVMH's long-term track record that exceeds market growth.

Notably, the industry has recently seen a wide slowdown as especially weak Chinese consumer confidence has pressured total luxury sales, with similar trends also seen in other markets. LVMH's revenues declined -1.7% and operating income by -14.2% in 2024 after a very long-term history of positive growth with luxury fashion, watches, and jewelry especially affected. Recently arising issues in the spirits industry have also contributed to a weaker performance, albeit very moderately, to LVMH's total earnings performance.

Positively, the 2025 outlook is brighter for the industry. Fashion and leather goods saw an approximated industry-wide -1% decline in 2024 sales, but is seen by Morgan Stanley to grow by at least 5% in 2025 based on a strong start to the year and an easing comparison base. Chinese consumer spending is also expected to start to gradually recover - after a small growth hiccup, LVMH's brands will likely compete in a better-growing industry in 2025.

Valuation

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model for LVMH's stock to estimate a fair value. I estimate an organic revenue CAGR of 4.1% in the next decade from 2024 to 2034, slightly below the overall luxury goods market's - the weaker position in watches and jewelry, and the spirit industry's overall weakness make LVMH's growth outlook slightly worse. LVMH has dominated in luxury fashion, but further market share gains are offset by Hermès' incredible strength.

The luxury goods market's recovery should improve LVMH's margin profile very slightly - as a base scenario estimate, I estimate the EBIT margin to reach a 24% level from 23.1% in 2024.

LVMH should also generate very healthy cash flow, as the company has overall moderate capital expenditures compared to its massive size.

The estimates put LVMH's fair value estimate at $737.7, very near the stock price at the time of writing; I believe that LVMH stock gives an option to buy a position in the luxury goods market at a fair price, but not bringing a skewed risk-to-reward in either direction.

The stock's 17.0 EV/EBITDA is also in line with most luxury goods peers', while Hermès has earned a massive premium in its valuation due to its performance.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.32%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 5.35% interest rate, annualized, from H2 interest expenses and debt. I estimate a very moderate 8% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year EUR swap yield of 2.41% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 5.13% is Professor Aswath Damodaran's most recent January estimates for France. Yahoo Finance estimates LVMH's beta at 0.99. With a liquidity premium of 0.1%, the cost of equity stands at 7.59% and the WACC at 7.32%.

Takeaway

LVMH is the undeniable king of luxury fashion with few companies being able to rival the giant - only Hermès has recently been able to put an end to rapid market share gains, after LVMH previously failed in a takeover attempt of Hermès over a decade ago. At the same time, LVMH's positions in other luxury goods are more contested, leading to worse performances in for example, jewelry, watches, and spirits. With the company looking to capture industry growth, LVMH's earnings prospects are improving after subdued consumer spending during 2024 as the luxury fashion market is estimated to recover.

The stock's valuation ultimately prices in a fair performance from the luxury conglomerate, and as such, I initiate LVMH at a Hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BITI: One Of The Easiest And Lowest-Risk Ways To Short Bitcoin
data.description:
I have a very useful and successful momentum formula that just flipped to a buy signal on ProShares Shrt Bitcoin ETF (NYSEARCA:BITI). This fund uses futures contracts to create a short position that will benefit owners if Bitcoin declines.

So, if you have been searching for a way to play a correction or bear market in the bubble move for Bitcoin, BITI is the plain-vanilla choice for retail investors inside a normal brokerage portfolio (usually available in IRA and 401-k accounts).

ProShares Short Bitcoin Background

ProShares opened this ETF for business in June 2022, once futures were approved for trading. After the wicked "inverse" decline in the fund, going in the opposite direction of Bitcoin's monster gains of 2023-24, total assets under management have fallen to $98 million. The fund does charge 1.03% in fees annually to manage its holdings and list the fund.

The ProShares website explains the investment objective as this,

ProShares Short Bitcoin ETF seeks daily investment results, before fees and expenses, that correspond to the inverse (-1x) of the daily performance of the Bloomberg Bitcoin Index.

The website also does a good job of exploring the risk of investment loss, which has been a regular occurrence since Bitcoin only seems to rise in price the last couple of years.

ETF holdings are quite simple to understand as of Friday's close (February 21st, 2025), with two futures contract expirations shorted, alongside cash held as collateral to create the inverse -1x position. The good news in today's financial market structure is cash investments do earn interest for ETF owners.

The bad news (and only realistic drawback to holding a BITI stake outside of Bitcoin prices continuing to zigzag higher) is Bitcoin futures participants put a nice premium value on contracts further out in time in anticipation of new cryptocurrency gains. This "contango" price condition costs extra money to generate BITI's -1x leverage, with monthly rollovers hurting the value of BITI. It has to do with crypto investment sentiment being uber-bullish today. If conventional wisdom flips to bearishness, with futures preparing for a bust, the opposite situation of futures selling for a discount to spot prices would appear (backwardation).

BITI Performance vs. Bitcoin

The best way to describe how this short ETF works for investors in the real world is through a review of past trading. The ETF has paid out a dividend from cash interest coming in the door at a faster clip than management fees are being expensed (since December 2022). So, I will use total return comparisons vs. Bitcoin price changes.

The main advantage of owning BITI vs. shorting futures directly is the design limits losses to -100% of your invested capital. If you are wrong on a short Bitcoin position in futures, you can lose many times your initial investment, where your broker requires you to add new money to keep your position. This concept is visible on the performance chart since inception below. Against the +368% Bitcoin gain since June 2022, BITI has declined just -87.6% in price. I know that's not the kind of loss anyone shoots for, but the idea of "limited" losses is fully on display.

That's not to say upside is limited in BITI. In fact, if Bitcoin were to collapse in value during 2025, the daily rebalance design of BITI could actually help compound gains in your favor, especially over shorter spans between futures contract rollovers. I am charting periods of sharp Bitcoin decline vs. BITI's oversized gains below.

Latest Performance Stats

In fact, BITI has performed quite admirably over the last four weeks, as the Trump-inspired euphoria in Bitcoin seems to be ebbing.

In general, the daily rebalance feature alongside cash interest have helped BITI to "outperform" its basic 1:1 design over the past year (albeit with major losses for the buy-and-hold crowd owning it greater than three months).

Final Thoughts

In conclusion, if you are bullish on Bitcoin, please avoid this product. However, if you are looking for an angle to bet on a Bitcoin correction or even an all-out bust in 2025, BITI should absolutely be on your research list.

Again, my plan is to buy a BITI position early next week, as one of my favorite momentum trading formulas is screaming now is a solid risk/reward moment to do so. An important trigger for my formula is a security's price getting back above its 50-day moving average, after suffering a major decline. While not expressly part of the formula, having rising trends in the Accumulation/Distribution Line, Negative Volume Index and On Balance Volume indicators over the past month are also a positive development.

I cannot guarantee any type of positive return or trading profit, but the odds seem to favor at least a minor drop in Bitcoin over coming weeks, with a bump higher in BITI the likely reaction. It's entirely possible I will take a quick +10% to +20% gain in BITI, if such appears into March.

Historically, holding this ETF longer than a few months has not outlined serious gains during Bitcoin's boom move in price. And, Bitcoin futures contract premiums do pose a stiff headwind currently in the marketplace. To achieve a +30% or better return in BITI, Bitcoin would have to drop by around the same amount, which may happen eventually, if and when Bitcoin busts.

To me, this idea is more of a trade. I do not suggest holding BITI longer than several months. If you will, investors can just assume a defined sell date is appropriate, like a stop-loss idea, 6–8 weeks in the future. At that time, you sell your position, no matter the profit/loss level or your personal outlook for Bitcoin. Having an exit plan in leveraged ETFs is just as (if not more) critical as entering a buy decision.

As a consequence, I have a 12-month outlook of Hold for the ProShares Short Bitcoin ETF. In addition, I would not be surprised by some sort of loss being the final destination for BITI trust holders by the end of 2025.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Revaluing U.S. Gold Reserves While Paying Down Debt? What A Concept
data.description:
If the whispers of revaluing the US gold reserves prove true, it could be an instant boon to the peoples' (currently debt-addled) asset base

[edit] Per the article's comments, a reader asks how a revaluation of gold helps pay down debt unless you sell the gold? My answer: "I am not talking about paying down debt by selling gold. I am talking about paying down debt after addressing government waste. Two separate things; debt and the peoples' assets (including gold). I should have made that clearer, I guess."

This article simply focuses on a couple of economically important areas and policies currently being floated. I am trying not to be political, which can be difficult in these times.

While you'll never see me hooting and hollering in a red hat, I note that amid the good and the bad in the rubble of Trump's economic and societal deconstruction, there are potential positives in play. Profound potential positives to a purist economic type like me.

I realize there will be pain among innocents during the deconstruction. But my job is to call the truth as I see it or the potential truths as I see them. There is a lot of bad happening right now, but there is a lot of good too. Potentially mind-boggling [economic] good, as we may see with the benefit of hindsight a year or two from now.

If there is any truth (it's politics and the media, after all) to the story about revaluing gold reserves, a combination of that and the dog named DOGE chewing through the books of government agencies looking for waste would be a revolutionary improvement, in theory. *

Revaluing US Gold Reserves, Cutting Waste/Paying Down Debt

Imagine that, a heretofore consumerist, over-financialized society actually paying down debt and reestablishing its asset base, its VALUE baseline, with the aid of its gold reserves. What a concept.

Today gold's free market price is over $2900/oz.

Pay down debt and value gold? Those are two things that I and other gold bug have advocated for decades. An old-fashioned notion in the face of the mighty debt-fueled consumer and the debt-money printing Federal Reserve system behind said consumer. Greed at any price.

Consider this new would-be era where tidal waves of government waste would be addressed (allowing debt paydown) and the nation's treasure of gold revalued to what the market says it should be valued at.

A Third Tier; Re-Shoring of Industry

Now add the re-shoring of industries that were sacrificed so that the financialized economy could increase the peoples' debt while magnificently enriching the already rich and powerful through the dark art of Keynesian finance. How can any of these three things be bad? I ask you.

For decades, the financialized, debt-backed US economy sacrificed its industrial base in service to the quick buck. Quick and BIG bucks raked in by Wall Street and its various outposts on the Main Streets of America (perhaps even your local financial adviser's shop, maybe itself a tentacle of a big firm).

The financialized economy has greatly increased the divide between the haves and those who, you know, don't have. Those going paycheck to paycheck. It is the gift that the financialized system and the Federal Reserve behind it have kept on giving (to the privileged asset owner class) at the expense of robo-trending national debt.

The Keynesian system, many years down the road and past its effective expiration date.

What thing looks like the other thing?

Gross Domestic Product is fine.

National Debt is fine too, if you are among the national minority "asset owner" class who have been exponentially enriched by the debt leveraged Ponzi scheme that is the engine of the chart above.

What thing looks like the other thing? Thing1 is driven by Thing 2. Thing 2 is not sustainable. Hence, in the current system, Thing 1 is not sustainable either, unless major changes are made.

Major changes are being made. Let's hope that there are more of the right ones than wrong ones, of which to my eye there will be plenty.

*In practice, there are a lot of moving parts, and there are a lot of resistance and legalities.

data.title: Ariel Investments International Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. All performance assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, and represents returns of the Investor Class shares. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Performance data current to the most recent month-end for Ariel International Fund may be obtained by visiting our website, arielinvestments.com. For the period ended December 31, 2024 the average annual total returns of Ariel International Fund (Investor Class) for the 1-, 5, and 10-year periods were +5.36%, +2.75%, and +3.43%, respectively.

Markets worldwide defied expectations in 2024 led by the dominating performance of U.S. mega-cap technology companies and investor enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI) themed stocks. However, persistent challenges in the Eurozone, including uncertainty around interest rates, concerns surrounding the new U.S. administration’s policies as well as a strengthening U.S. dollar drove the MSCI ACWI, MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. and MSCI EAFE Indices into the red. China’s economy also ended the year sluggishly, with September’s stimulus efforts losing steam and the recovery in consumer spending turning elusive. Although uncertainty and volatility are likely to remain elevated, the patient investor knows “stock prices trade on fundamentals. And when those solid fundamentals shine through, share prices rise. 1” Against this backdrop, Ariel International Fund declined -5.11% in the quarter, outperforming both its primary and secondary benchmark, the MSCI EAFE and MSCI ACWI ex-US Indices, which fell -8.11% and -7.60%, respectively. Over the trailing one-year period, Ariel International Fund rose +5.36%, outperforming the MSCI EAFE Index’s +3.82% return, but slightly lagging the +5.53% gain posted by the MSCI ACWI ex-US Index.

Ariel’s non-consensus approach seeks to identify undervalued, out-of-favor, franchises that are misunderstood and therefore mispriced. Ariel International Fund is overweight Consumer Discretionary, Utilities, Information Technology and Financials. The portfolio is meaningfully underweight Industrials, Energy, Consumer Staples and Communication Services, as well as lacks exposure to Materials. At the sector level, our stock selection within Health Care, Real Estate and Consumer Staples as well as our avoidance of Materials were the largest sources of positive attribution. By comparison, our Consumer Discretionary holdings, allocation to currency forwards and positioning within Communication Services were the greatest performance detractors during the quarter.

Germany-based kidney dialysis services and products provider, Fresenius Medical Care AG (OTCPK:FMCQF)(FMS) advanced in the period following solid quarterly earnings results driven by an inflection in the U.S. market as treatment volumes returned to positive growth. Subsequently, management upgraded its 2024 outlook and reaffirmed guidance for 2025. Meanwhile, the company continues to successfully execute on its goal to exit non-core assets in Latin America and invest in Europe and Asia. Additionally, a new ruling from the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) for 2025 that increases payments to end-stage renal disease facilities further aided shares. At current valuation levels, we believe the Fresenius remains well-positioned from a risk/reward standpoint.

New holding, Italian bank, Banca Monte dei Paschi (OTCPK:BMDPF) also increased following a quarterly earnings beat and subsequent raise in full year guidance. The Italian government also sold a portion of its stake in the firm further aiding share price appreciation. We believe the bank can provide long-term earnings stability through its recent actions to reduce interest rate sensitivity and improve asset quality, thereby enhancing profitability and the balance sheet. Additionally, the bank's increased payout ratio to 75% and attractive cash yield—the highest among European Union banks—bolsters our positive outlook. Furthermore, we think a favorable interest rate outlook alongside the company’s solid capital profile and robust trading book as well as the potential for consolidation in the Italian banking sector presents additional upside potential.

Additionally, Emaar Properties PJSC, a property investment and development company in the MENA region, traded higher over the period on strong earnings results, highlighted by record quarterly residential sales, healthy margins and robust free cash flow. Additionally, the company announced an attractive long-term dividend policy to return capital to shareholders. Looking ahead, we believe Emaar Properties will continue to benefit from the post COVID recovery in Dubai’s real estate market, which has been amplified by a large number of expatriates entering the country.

In contrast, French multinational tire manufacturer, Michelin (CDGE) was a notable underperformer in the period due to a challenging macro environment. Weaker-than-expected consumer demand caused a sharp decline in volumes resulting in to a top- and bottom-line earnings miss. Subsequently, management cut 2024 guidance and announced plans to close two of its factories by 2026 to reduce costs. Nonetheless, we remain enthusiastic about Michelin's strong global competitive position and pricing strategy. In our view, shares are trading at a large discount to historical multiples despite a stronger balance sheet, higher free cash flow generation and attractive returns of capital to shareholders.

French pharmaceutical company, Sanofi (SNY)(OTCPK:SNYNF) also underperformed. The new administration’s decision to appoint a well-known vaccine critic as the future Secretary of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) weighed on pharmaceutical shares industrywide. In our view, the sell-off is an overreaction, as the vaccine business is a small part of Sanofi’s total revenue. Moreover, vaccine effectiveness is supported by strong scientific evidence and real-world data, particularly for young, elderly and at-risk patients. At current valuation levels, we view Sanofi's vaccine business and its Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) Antibody as underappreciated and believe the immunology pipeline continues to be overlooked. Also, we remain optimistic Dupixent will be a key long-term growth driver.

Finally, China-based E-commerce company, JD.com, Inc. (OTCPK:JDCMF)(JD) detracted from performance in the period. The stock came under pressure as some investors took profits on solid earnings performance, while others became concerned with the implications tariffs could have on the Chinese economy. In our view, this share price action runs counter to the company's solid business fundamentals. The home appliance trade-in program and popular shopping event, Singles' Day, generated significant consumer spending across various product categories. Additionally, the company’s strategic decision to diversify general merchandise product offerings, expand its third-party marketplace business and monetize advertising streams continues to aid the top- and bottom-lines. Despite the near-term noise, we continue to view the company’s strategic positioning favorably and like JD.com’s long-term growth prospects.

In addition to purchasing Italian bank, Banca Monte dei Paschi, we initiated seven new positions during the quarter.

We added global bank and financial services provider, Barclays PLC (OTCPK:BCLYF)(BCS). We expect shares to benefit from a recovery in global capital markets and net interest income (NII) growth driven by macroeconomic hedging and asset flows. The bank is also planning to expand its investment banking advisory business. Moreover, its U.S. credit card business presents opportunities for either a potential sale or a quicker earnings recovery. Taken together, we see a reasonable path for Barclays to pursue its return targets, which include the distribution of £3 billion and £10 billion to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases between 2024 and 2026 and achieving a return on tangible common equity of about 12%.

We initiated a position in specialty insurance company, Beazley plc (OTCPK:BZLYF)(OTCPK:BZLEY). The firm’s diversified portfolio offerings include commercial property, specialty risk and cyber insurance. We find the growth and profitability profile of its property segment to be attractive as 100% of its business in North America is being generated by the booming Excess and Surplus (E&S) market—an area that caters to non-traditional, high-risk insurance needs. Cyber also presents an exciting structural growth opportunity for the company, particularly since insurance penetration of information assets is low.

Looking ahead, we find Beazley’s earnings outlook to be attractive, supported by its robust business mix, solid margins and improving capital profile.

We also bought leading European telecommunications services operator, Orange SA (OTC:ORANY). The company offers mobile, fixed-line and broadband services to roughly 300 million customers across 26 countries. Orange SA has a strong market presence in France and Spain and is expanding operations across the Middle East and Africa (MEA). We believe positive subscriber trends and rational price increases across these markets will support long term revenue growth. For instance, Orange is gaining market share on its network and service quality in France; benefitting from merger synergies and monetizing the fiber business in Spain; and offering mobile data services to significantly under-penetrated regions in MEA. Meanwhile, as the company completes its fiber-roll-out in France, we expect higher profitability, free cash flow generation and increasing shareholder returns.

We purchased Redeia Corp SA (OTCPK:RDEIF)(OTCPK:RDEIY), a Spain-based electricity provider. We believe Redeia’s ability to generate robust earnings growth with further upside from additional regulatory drivers is currently being underappreciated by the market.

We added shares of leading pure-play producer of memory semiconductor products, SK Hynix Inc. (OTCPK:HXSCF) In our view, SK Hynix is well positioned to benefit from increased deployment of its leading-edge High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) products, which are experiencing rapid demand growth to power Artificial Intelligence (AI).

We initiated a position in Sega Sammy Holdings Inc. (OTCPK:SGAMY)(OTCPK:SGAMF), a Japanese holding company specializing in the development of video games and the production of pachinko and pachislot machines. We believe the company is poised to deliver higher earnings, driven by robust growth within its video game segment. This is largely due to the company’s new multiplatform storytelling strategy centered around the firm’s core intellectual property: Sonic, Atlus, and Like a Dragon.

We believe it will help drive sales of both old and new titles, thereby boosting margins. Meanwhile, the pachinko and pachislot segment remains a profitable and stable cash flow generator for the company.

Lastly, we added Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets ETF (VEA) as a cash alternative during the quarter.

By comparison, we successfully exited German reinsurer, Munich Re., international supplier of systems and equipment in aerospace, defense, and security, Safran SA (OTCPK:SAFRF)(OTCPK:SAFRY) as well as high-tech solutions and services provider in the defense and satellite space, Thales SA (OTCPK:THLEF)(OTCPK:THLLY) on valuation. We also sold out of British home and auto insurer, Direct Line Insurance Group Plc (OTCPK:DIISF)(OTCPK:DIISY) and Korean-based leading manufacturer of mobile phones and smartphones, Samsung Electronics Company, Ltd (OTCPK:SSNLF) to pursue more compelling opportunities.

The stock market dynamics of 2024 highlight a transformative shift in the global economy, fueled by advancements in artificial intelligence and geopolitical fragmentation. While the narrow ascent of AI themed stocks have driven market returns in recent years, opportunities should broaden to companies utilizing AI to create real-world efficiencies. As we head into 2025, there may be an over-reliance on the U.S. stock market to drive returns. With uncertainty surrounding rate cuts as well as the policy agenda for the new US administration, we expect heightened market volatility. We see this environment as conducive for active managers, whose knowledge, expertise and forward thinking enables them to look beyond the short-term noise and identify opportunities.

Accordingly, we are finding many mispriced global and international stocks trading at a discount to their U.S. counterparts. Their valuation is attractive, profitability less vulnerable and balance sheets remain strong. Against this backdrop, our international and global portfolios are well-positioned to deliver stronger returns over the long run.

Footnote

1 Hobson, Mellody and John W. Rogers Jr. “What the Stock market Taught Us This Year: Don’t Fall for These Investing Traps.” Wall Street Journal, 5 December 2023.

Investments in non-U.S. securities may underperform and may be more volatile than comparable U.S. stocks because of the risks involving non-U.S. economies, markets, political systems, regulatory standards, currencies and taxes. The use of currency derivatives and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) may increase investment losses and expenses, and create more volatility. Investments in emerging markets present additional risks, such as difficulties in selling on a timely basis and at an acceptable price. The intrinsic value of the stocks in which the Fund invests may never be recognized by the broader market. The Fund is often concentrated in fewer sectors than its benchmarks, and its performance may suffer if these sectors underperform the overall stock market. Investing in equity stocks is risky and subject to the volatility of the markets.
