data.title: Chevron Stock's Outlook Is Uncertain At This Point (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Thesis

Chevron Corporation (NYSE:CVX) (NEOE:CHEV:CA) stock has been dead money for the past year as 52 week gains stand at a mere 2%. In the below analysis, I determine that the technicals show a strong outlook. But the positivity fades when I analyze the fundamentals, as most recent earnings showed mixed results. In addition, after analyzing various valuation multiples, I conclude that the stock is moderately overvalued currently, making the fundamental setup unattractive for the stock. Therefore, since the technicals and fundamentals highly disagree on Chevron's outlook, I believe a hold rating is appropriate until there is a clearer consensus.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

Chevron stock is currently in both a longer-term uptrend and a nearer term downtrend. The uptrend dates back to September, while the downtrend started in late November. As these trend lines converge, the stock will be forced to either breakdown or breakout, and so later this year there could be a make-or-break moment for the stock.

Support

In addition to the uptrend line, there are two other significant sources of support. The nearer one is at 152 and represents heavy resistance in October. The other support area is at 147 as it was the floor for the stock also in October and was key support at the beginning of February.

Resistance

Other than the downtrend line, there is only one other major source of resistance for the stock in this time frame. It is at 163 and represents the late November to early December peak in the stock. That was a six-month-high and there was quite a bit of trading action at that level before the down move, and so it could be resistance again moving forward.

Takeaway

Overall, I would say the chart is slightly net positive. The uptrend in Chevron stock dates back further, and there is also more support than resistance currently. Therefore, the odds favour an upside breakout rather than a breakdown as the trend lines converge.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average (SMA)

The 50-day SMA had a death cross with the 200-day SMA earlier this month, but I would consider it a non-signal currently. Since the crossover, the gap between the SMAs has been unable to expand meaningfully, and the stock is now far above both SMAs. Therefore, this bearish crossover is likely a false signal and I find the message here to be long term technical indecision as the SMAs have remained very close together since early December.

Exponential Moving Average (EMA)

The 9 day EMA crossed below the 21 day EMA momentarily earlier this month and crossed back above quickly. This is an indication of resilient bulls in the stock. Furthermore, the gap between the EMAs is currently expanding, showing that near term bullish momentum is accelerating at a healthy pace.

Bollinger Bands

After the stock plunged below the midline in late January, it was able to reclaim that level earlier this month as a signal of strength. In addition, the midline acted as support only days ago. This shows that the longer-term uptrend is likely stronger than the nearer term downtrend, and so again it seems more likely that the stock will breakout on the upside.

Takeaway

I believe the moving averages show a net positive technical outlook for Chevron stock. The SMAs generally show uncertainty, but the EMAs show accelerating near term bullishness and the Bollinger Bands show that the odds are in the bulls' favour for an upside breakout.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD)

The MACD had a bullish crossover earlier this month, but the gap between the lines has remained relatively small and so there seems to be some persistent bears in the stock as of late. The gap has expanded by a small margin in recent days as shown by the bullish histogram and so there has been a modest increase in bullishness.

Relative Strength Index (RSI)

The RSI is currently at 59.35. This indicator reclaimed the 50 level earlier this month, reflecting that the bulls have taken control of the stock. The RSI recorded a six-month high in mid-January, eclipsing the November highs in the indicator, despite the stock being unable to best its late November high thus far. While the stock has receded since then, it nonetheless shows there has been strength in Chevron.

Stochastics

The last major crossover in the stochastics occurred in early February, and it was a bullish one. Since then, the %K has bounced off the %D as a sign of resilient bulls, and there was also a false bearish crossover just very recently. The gap between the lines is currently at a healthy margin and both lines have entered into the 80+ zone, showing that the bulls are in firm control of the stock.

Takeaway

In my view, these indicators as a whole show a positive technical setup for Chevron. The MACD is in a bullish state overall, the RSI is well above 50 and recently showed long-term strength, while the stochastics reflects that the bulls have a strong grasp on the stock.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Revenue

Chevron released their 2024 Q4 earnings back at the end of January. They reported Sales and other operating revenues of $48.334 billion, down slightly from $48.933 billion in the prior year period. As you can see in the chart above, for revenue growth there was a slight improvement over the previous quarter, but overall revenue growth remains sluggish and is at below average levels. Their total revenues and other incomes figure beat expectations by $3.82 billion.

Earnings Per Share

For EPS, they reported a diluted GAAP figure of $1.84, up significantly from the year ago quarter's $1.22. As shown above, EPS growth had a meaningful acceleration versus the previous quarter and represents the best growth since late 2022. Even though this figure looks like it is still below the three-year average due to the high percentage growth in 2022, the current over 50% growth rate is impressive. GAAP EPS did miss expectations, however, as it underperformed estimates by $0.10.

Cash Flow From Operations (CFO)

A key figure for Chevron is the cash flow from operations figure, and they showed rather worrying results. They reported $8.7 billion in CFOs, down significantly YoY from $12.4 billion. In the above chart, it is shown that CFO growth has taken a turn for the worse as the growth rate neared zero for the prior quarters. This -30% growth rate is the worst since late 2023 and represents far below average results compared to the past three years.

Takeaway

I believe this earnings report was overall mixed, with key metrics differing in their directions. Revenue results were relatively neutral as sluggish growth continues. EPS results were quite promising, with a major acceleration in growth after more than a year of negative or near zero growth. However, CFO results were worrying as Q4 growth showed a significant turn for the worse.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio (P/S)

The P/S ratio is currently at 1.486 and is at slightly below average to around average levels of the past three years. Overall, I believe this ratio shows modest overvaluation in Chevron stock. As discussed above, currently revenue growth is at below average levels and so there is a mild disconnect between the ratio and the financials with this multiple near average levels. The energy sector's median P/S ratio is currently 1.36 and so Chevron is trading a modest premium to peers as well.

Price To Earnings Ratio (P/E)

The P/E ratio is at above average levels currently and has a reading of 16.27. In my view, this ratio is justified and shows fair valuation for Chevron stock. EPS growth showed significant improvement and is at the highest level since late 2022. Therefore, the above average level in the P/E ratio should be no surprise for investors. The median GAAP P/E ratio for the energy sector is currently 12.92 and so Chevron is trading at a moderate premium to peers according to this measure.

Price To Cash Flow From Operations Ratio (P/CFO)

The P/CFO ratio is also at above average levels and is currently at 9.121. Here is where I find significant overvaluation in Chevron stock. As discussed above, CFO growth has taken a major negative turn and is at its worst level since late 2023. These results are worrying for investors, especially since the P/CFO ratio is at above average levels. There is a clear disconnect between the ratio and the financials, leading me to believe Chevron is significantly overvalued according to this multiple. Furthermore, the energy sector's median P/CFO ratio is currently 5.4 and so Chevron is at a major premium to peers.

Takeaway

I conclude that Chevron is moderately overvalued at current levels. The P/S ratio showed modest overvaluation, as there was only a mild disconnect between the multiple and revenue growth. The P/E ratio generally reflected fair valuation, as its high level is justified by the impressive EPS growth. However, the P/CFO ratio showed major overvaluation in the stock, with the ratio being completely detached from reality. Therefore, I believe overall Chevron is moderately overvalued.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, I have determined that the technicals are very bullish for Chevron stock, as the majority of indications point towards strength moving forward. However, that is highly at odds with the fundamental setup. As discussed above, the most recent earning results were quite mixed, and the stock is currently overvalued as there was a major disconnect between the P/CFO ratio and the CFO growth rate. Therefore, with the two branches of analysis conflicting on their outlooks for Chevron, I believe the stock is only a hold currently.

data.title: LCLG: 12 Years Of High Fees And Poor Returns For This Innovative Growth ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This article explains why I've assigned a "sell" rating for the Logan Capital Broad Innovative Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:LCLG), an actively managed fund with a 0.96% expense ratio and a long track record of underperforming its benchmark, represented by the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF). Making matters worse is how its components are highly leveraged and don't offer the same growth potential as most low-cost alternatives. I'll highlight a few below for you to consider, and as always, I look forward to your comments afterward.

LCLG Overview

Performance Snapshot vs. Peers

Per its website, LCLG is benchmarked against the Russell 1000 Growth Index. IWF is the most popular ETF tracking this Index, but Vanguard's VONG offers a cheaper expense ratio (0.08% vs. 0.19%), and for these purposes, they're essentially the same fund. In this analysis, I will also evaluate LCLG against the Schwab U.S. Large Cap Growth ETF (SCHG), the SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 Growth ETF (SPYG), and the American Century U.S. Quality Growth ETF (QGRO), as profiled below.

LCLG's inception date is listed as June 28, 2012, but it only launched as an ETF on August 8, 2022, though it was permitted to retain the performance of its predecessor fund for the ten years prior due to the two having "substantially similar investment strategies." You might have noticed its 0.96% expense ratio sticks out for all the wrong reasons, and this is LCLG's first red flag. While it's certainly possible, an active manager can outperform a passive Index, shareholders wind up losing 13.66% of their gains to fees over ten years, assuming a 10% annual return. In contrast, you'll lose only 1.18% with VONG and 0.59% with SCHG and SPYG, according to this calculator.

This is a huge eight-ball that Logan Capital has placed its shareholders behind, and unfortunately, they have not delivered good results. As shown below, LCLG's total return since its inception was 94% worse than IWF (577% vs. 671%) and lagged behind SCHG, the most aggressive growth fund in this sample, by a massive 134%.

Importantly, the fee impact tells only part of the story. The fact that these differences are so huge indicates a problem with the overall strategy, whether that be poor stock selection or underweighting high-potential stocks in the name of improving diversification. I'll explore potential reasons for this shortly, but first, please consider the statistics I calculated based on the graph above compared to IWF.

  • LCLG underperformed by 1.18% on average between 2013 and 2024.
  • LCLG's best year was 2019, outperforming IWF by 3.04%.
  • LCLG's worst year was 2014, underperforming IWF by 7.61%.
  • LCLG's monthly win rate was 54.60% (83/152 months).
  • LCLG outperformed by 1.20% on average in winning months.
  • LCLG underperformed by 1.53% on average in losing months.
  • LCLG's standard deviation was 2.15% higher (17.80% vs. 15.64%)

These statistics paint a picture of an ETF that tends to select above-average risk stocks that can get into trouble in down markets. Things are fine in up marks, as LCLG has a solid 54.60% monthly win rate over IWF, but its high volatility can wipe out those gains quickly and make recovery more difficult. For example, LCLG lost 33.70% in the first nine months of 2022, which required a 50.83% gain just to break even. In contrast, IWF's 30.75% decline needed a 44.44% gain to break even, or 6.39% less. This is why it's important investors consider risk even for growth ETFs.

LCLG Analysis

Strategy Discussion

LCLG follows a proprietary strategy, but its website and prospectus provide some details about how its managers go about selecting high-potential "innovative" growth stocks. I've summarized the most important points from these sources below.

1. The target count is approximately 40-60 U.S. securities with a minimum $5 billion market capitalization selected from a universe of about 1,000.

2. Logan Capital's philosophy is that earnings growth is the most important driver of stock price growth. The firm's goal is to identify companies with "outstanding earnings growth due to factors such as superior pricing power, distribution channels, management, etc."

3. The research process is "bottom-up" and fundamentals-driven, meaning the firm evaluates companies based on their growth prospects, market expansion opportunities, and financial strength. From the website, the strategy "seeks companies that benefit from an economic tailwind" and can "deliver increased earnings tied to pricing power."

4. The firm also relies on macroeconomic statistics like GDP, interest rates, the yield curve, and inflation expectations to aid security analysis.

5. As a double-check, securities must have a strong technical profile that supports the firm's bottom-up research and macroeconomic view.

Overall, the strategy seems quite comprehensive, but since its proprietary, it's difficult to know which factors matter most to the managers. There is also no information provided on the fund's weighting scheme. It's important not to underestimate this feature because, oftentimes, only a handful of stocks drive an ETF's returns. Lately, it's NVIDIA (NVDA), and if you own an ETF that underweights that stock, there's a good chance you've underperformed.

Sector Allocation and Composition Differences vs. Peers

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between LCLG, IWF, SCHG, SPYG, and QGRO. One aspect that stands out is how LCLG underweights Technology stocks by 9.88% on average, with the offset primarily in Consumer Discretionary. This composition also indicates how the portfolio is pretty aggressive. There is very little allocated to defensive sectors like Consumer Staples, Health Care, and Utilities.

LCLG's top holdings include AppLovin (APP), Netflix (NFLX), and Broadcom (AVGO), and its top 25 holdings comprise 77.37% of the portfolio. As I mentioned earlier, I don't know the fund's weighting scheme, but I'm pretty certain managers just don't rebalance very often. According to my records, AppLovin was LCLG's #23 holding with a 1.53% allocation, but it's now the fund's top holding due to its 420% six-month price return.

Fundamentals By Top 25 Holdings

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for LCLG's top 25 holdings. I've also included summary metrics for IWF, SCHG, SPYG, and QGRO at the bottom.

Here are four key takeaways:

1. LCLG's weighted average market cap is $610 billion, but it has a wide range of sizes, consistent with the $5 billion minimum mentioned earlier. Mega-cap stocks like Apple (AAPL) and Meta Platforms (META) are complimented with small/mid-cap stocks like DICK'S Sporting Goods (DKS) and Lithia Motors (LAD), and while the weighted average market is still quite high, there's an argument to be made that LCLG is an all-cap growth fund. The same is true for QGRO, which allocates 30% to stocks with market caps below $50 billion.

2. LCLG has a weighted average 1.32 five-year beta, and based on its high historical one-year beta and high standard deviation, it's safe to say LCLG is an above-average risky fund.

3. Recall how LCLG's strategy emphasizes growth, but that's tough to reconcile with the figures presented in the table. I looked at three-year historical and one-year estimated sales and earnings per share growth rates, and in all cases, LCLG's rates trail its peers by 2-6%. There are several stocks with mid-single-digit estimated earnings growth rates, like United Rentals (URI), Fastenal (FAST), and Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL). Their historical rates aren't much better, and as a whole, LCLG's sector-adjusted growth score, which I derived from individual Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, is the weakest of the five, as follows:

  • LCLG: 5.67/10
  • IWF: 5.77/10
  • SCHG: 5.93/10
  • SPYG: 5.96/10
  • QGRO: 6.35/10

While LCLG's 27.65x forward P/E ratio is the most attractive, investors should consider QGRO as a lower-cost alternative. Its growth rates are much better, its forward P/E ratio is nearly the same at 28.84x, it's well-diversified, and, as we'll see next, its quality features are just as robust.

4. LCLG's selections, coincidentally, have the exact same 35.46% weighted average return on equity as IWF. However, their path to these high margins requires a closer look. Consider how a company's return on equity can be broken down into three parts using the DuPont Formula, as follows:

  • Net Income / Revenue (Net Margin)
  • Revenue / Average Assets (Asset Turnover)
  • Average Assets / Average Equity (Financial Leverage)

After canceling out some terms, we're left with Net Income / Average Equity, which is the formula for ROE. This breakdown allows us to see that companies can improve profits through leverage, and that's exactly the case for LCLG. Its selections have a weighted average 1.29 debt-equity ratio compared to 1.09 for IWF and less for the three others. This ratio is not quite the same as the leverage calculation above, but all you need to do is add one to the value to get a good approximation (e.g., 1.29 + 1 = 2.29), so it's still informative.

I also noticed LCLG's weighted average net margins are only 21.45% compared to 25.57% for IWF, so it's undoubtedly more dependent on debt and more sensitive to changes in interest rates. It's concerning, given how, as reported by Seeking Alpha, "year-ahead inflation expectations jumped up from 3.3% last month to 4.3% this month, the highest reading since November 2023 and marking two consecutive months of unusually large increases."

Overall, we see that LCLG's 9.27/10 sector-adjusted profit score is only marginally better than QGRO's 9.24/10 score and well behind IWF, SCHG, and SPYG. As noted in the earlier point, when you consider all that LCLG offers (growth, value, quality, diversification from mega-caps), QGRO simply does it better for a lower cost and less risk. That's also been the case since its launch on September 10, 2018.

Investment Recommendation

LCLG and its predecessor mutual fund have lagged their benchmark by 1.18% on average per year since 2013, with greater volatility. To be fair, this was partly due to lesser allocations in mega-cap stocks in favor of some small-cap growth names. However, my analysis revealed that LCLG's selections have greater financial leverage than its peers, and QGRO is the superior choice for those looking to save on fees while diversifying away from the mega-caps. As a result, I've assigned LCLG a "sell" rating. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway Unlikely To Beat Market In 2025: No Buybacks, No Big Plays
data.description:
I downgraded NYSE:BRK.B stock to "Hold" in early 2024, arguing that despite Berkshire Hathaway's (NYSE:BRK.A) strong operating performance, Buffett’s capital allocation strategy has been too conservative for my liking, with Berkshire being a net seller of equities for multiple quarter, missing a major market rally, while the conglomerate's cash position is constantly swelling to new record heights. With that said, since I have shifted to a neutral position on BRK, shares have started to underperform against the broader U.S. equity markets. For the trailing twelve months, BRK stock is up only about 17%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) gained 21%, respectively.

Now, looking at 2025, I don't think BRK shares can shift gears to outperform again, as Buffett continues to reduce exposure to equities and avoids buybacks of his own stock amidst an arguably rich valuation.

BRK Delivers Solid Operating Performance

Q4 was relatively good for Berkshire Hathaway, from an operating perspective: During the period spanning from October through end of December, Berkshire generated $94.9 billion of revenue up 2% YoY, and very much in line with consensus estimates, according to data collected by Refinitiv. During the same period, operating income jumped to $14.5 billion for the quarter, a notable 71% YoY increase compared to Q4 2023. Strong results were mostly supported by the insurance business, with operating earnings in the underwriting and investment income up 400% YoY (3.4 billion) and 48% YoY ($4.1 billion), respectively. On that note, Buffett commented:

In general, property-casualty (“P/C”) insurance pricing strengthened during 2024, reflecting a major increase in damage from convective storms. Climate change may have been announcing its arrival. However, no “monster” event occurred during 2024. Someday, any day, a truly staggering insurance loss will occur – and there is no guarantee that there will be only one per annum.

At the same time, Berkshire's operating performance was supported by a large gain of income from the conglomerate's large and growing Treasury investments. The railroad business and the energy business performed relatively stable YoY, bringing in operating earnings of about $1.29 billion and $0.3 billion, respectively.

Another Quarter Of Reducing Equity Exposure

As I highlighted in the introduction of this article, I have been concerned about Buffett's decision to reduce exposure to equities in this roaring bull market. In Q4, Buffett's risk appetite has not changed. According to the Q4 print, Buffett sold $143.4 billion worth of stock in 2024, while purchases were only $9.2 billion. With this, 2024 marked an acceleration in Buffett's selling activity compared to 2023, when he sold $40 billion worth of stocks and about $16.5 billion. For reference, in both 2023 and 2024 the S&P 500 scored 20% YoY price appreciation gains.

Personally, I don't think Buffett's cautious approach towards equities will be successful in 2025. In fact, heading into the new year, I wrote an article titled "stocks may melt up like it is 1999" (but I may be wrong, of course):

Wrapping it up, the S&P 500 has crushed it with massive gains in 2023 and 2024, and while some market participants are projecting a more muted 5% upside in 2025, I’m not convinced the rally is over. If anything, the AI boom feels a lot like the internet explosion of the 1990s—but even bigger, thanks to AI’s ability to supercharge margins and productivity across nearly every industry. With top holdings set for 18% revenue growth and political tailwinds like deregulation and tax cuts adding fuel, there’s a strong case for earnings growth outpacing revenues.

I argued that applying a 24-27x multiple on the $280 EPS estimate for the S&P 500, according to data collected by Refinitiv, the implied fair valuation for the S&P 500 should be around 6700-7500 (notable upside of 13-27%).

I am also concerned that Buffett has decided not to buy its own stock (share repurchases either). For FY, share repurchases totaled $2.9 billion, with no incremental buybacks in Q4. Historically, Buffett has been very clear in his communication to investors that his philosophy towards buybacks is very value-driven - he won't buy back shares when he thinks the valuation is stretched.

My Top 3 Takeaways From The Letter

As Buffett always does at the end of the year, he wrote a letter to shareholders. For me, three big lessons stand out. First, own your mistakes and fix them fast. Buffett openly admits he’s made bad investment calls and hiring mistakes, but what really hurts is delaying action—what Charlie Munger called "thumb-sucking." Investors often cling to bad stocks, hoping they’ll bounce back, instead of cutting their losses and moving on. Second, great leadership beats fancy credentials. Buffett couldn’t care less where someone went to school—what matters is whether they can run a business well. Pete Liegl, the RV king who built Forest River into a massive success, didn’t have an Ivy League degree, but his instincts and work ethic made Berkshire billions. Investors should focus on strong management and business fundamentals, not just impressive resumes. Finally, a few big winners make all the difference. Buffett reminds us that "a single winning decision can make a breathtaking difference over time." GEICO, Ajit Jain, and even his lifelong partnership with Munger prove that getting just a handful of things right can outweigh dozens of mistakes. For investors, the message is clear: don’t dwell on small losses, focus on finding great businesses, and let compounding do the heavy lifting.

Valuation: Looks Overvalued

Zooming in on estimates for Berkshire Hathaway, I use the consensus EPS forecast to build a residual earnings valuation model. With projected EPS for FY 2025, FY 2026, and FY 2027 at approximately $20.1, $21, and $21.9, respectively—combined with an 8% cost of equity and a 2.0% terminal growth rate—I estimate Berkshire Hathaway fair implied target valuation at around $289, projecting about 40% downside from current levels. With that, I perfectly understand Buffett's reluctance to buy back shares at current levels.

Below also the sensitivity table varying cost of equity (row) and terminal growth (column).

What's The Upside In 2025?

I am not necessarily bearish on BRK stock in 2025. In my view, Buffett still sits on a portfolio of high-quality stocks, which should do well against a solid macro backdrop. At the same time, Berkshire's massive investments in Treasury securities can be considered relatively safe, they offer a solid 5% income, and they give optionality for shifting back to equities in the case of a market drop. That said, however, I am concerned about Berkshire's set up for a change to outperform the S&P 500 in 2025. As I have highlighted here, the fastest growing stocks in the S&P 500 universe are the magnificent seven names, excluding Apple. At the same time, and as underscored with Q4 results, investors shouldn't expect that share buybacks will juice returns in 2025: In my view, Buffett would likely want to see BRK shares at least ~20% lower before he thinks the valuation set-up is attractive for buybacks (my personal view, anchored also on the residual earnings model presented above). This is not where Berkshire is invested currently. So I don't think that Berkshire is positioned to outperform in 2025.

Investor Takeaway

Berkshire Hathaway delivered a strong Q4 2024, with operating income surging 71% YoY growing to $14.5 billion, driven by strong operating gains in insurance underwriting ($3.4 billion, +400% YoY) and investment income ($4.1 billion, +48% YoY). Despite the solid macro backdrop, Buffett remained cautious on equities: For FY 2024, Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway dumped $143.4 billion in stocks while purchasing just $9.2 billion, an acceleration of his defensive stance from 2023. With no Q4 buybacks and only $2.9 billion repurchased for the year, Buffett signaled that Berkshire's stock may be overvalued. The overvaluation thought is in line with my residual earnings model, which estimates BRK.B's fair implied value at $289 per share. As financial wisdom extracted from the investor letter, I liked how Buffett reinforced 3 key investing principles—admit mistakes quickly, prioritize leadership over credentials, and focus on a few big winners. I remain "Hold" rated.
